content.text: What it is

Portfolio kill criteria are the pre-defined, quantifiable conditions under which an AI use case or project is removed from the active portfolio and its resources are redistributed. Kill criteria are established before the project begins — not during delivery — and specify the objective conditions (missed milestones, unmet performance thresholds, eliminated business context) that trigger a sunset decision. A sunset rule is the governance process that activates when kill criteria are met: who reviews the evidence, who has authority to sunset the project, what happens to the team and the artifacts, and how the decision is communicated.
Why it matters

AI programs accumulate zombie projects: use cases that have not been formally killed but also are not delivering value, continuing to consume resources and management attention without generating returns. Without pre-defined kill criteria, sunetting a project becomes a political act — someone must advocate for killing something they or a senior executive sponsored, creating disincentives to act decisively. Kill criteria convert the sunset decision from a political negotiation into a governance procedure: when the criteria are met, the process activates, regardless of who sponsored the project. This also makes portfolio investment more disciplined: teams know from the start that their project will be evaluated against objective criteria, not just political factors.
Benefits
Prevents zombie projects from consuming resources: a project that meets kill criteria is sunset on schedule, freeing its resources for Invest-tier use cases.
Removes political friction from sunset decisions: kill criteria make the decision mechanical rather than political, which is more defensible and faster.
Creates delivery accountability: teams know from day one that there are objective conditions under which their project will be stopped, which improves planning discipline and honest reporting.
Enables portfolio rebalancing: freed resources from sunset projects can be immediately redirected to the next priority in the portfolio rather than sitting idle pending a political decision.
Reduces sunk-cost bias: pre-defined criteria make it harder to argue 'we've invested too much to stop now' — the criteria were agreed before the investment was made.
Trade-offs and limitations

Criteria must be set carefully: kill criteria that are too stringent sunset projects that are temporarily underperforming but genuinely valuable; criteria that are too lenient allow zombie projects to persist.
Some value is intangible: a use case that does not meet its financial ROI criteria may still be generating valuable organizational learning; kill criteria must include mechanisms to credit this.
Context changes: kill criteria appropriate at project start may be inappropriate 18 months later when the business context has changed; criteria should be reviewed at each phase gate.

Not all projects should have the same criteria: a Research tier use case should have different kill criteria than an Invest tier use case — Research is killed if it generates no useful insights, not if it fails to deliver ROI.
How it works

Kill criteria are defined in three categories. Performance criteria specify thresholds that the use case must achieve by defined dates: minimum benefit realization rate (e.g., 50% of projected annual benefit by 18 months), minimum user adoption rate, minimum model quality metric. Viability criteria specify conditions under which the underlying assumptions of the use case are invalidated: the data source becomes unavailable, regulatory prohibition is enacted, the business process the AI was supporting is eliminated. Investment efficiency criteria specify thresholds on the ratio of delivery cost to delivered value: if the running total investment exceeds three times the realized benefit at any review point, the project enters a sunset review.

Sunset rules define the governance process when criteria are met. A criterion met for the first time triggers a remediation period — typically 60 days — during which the team must demonstrate a credible path to criterion recovery. If the criterion is still not met after the remediation period, a formal sunset review is triggered. The sunset review is conducted by the steering committee and produces one of three outcomes: sunset (resources are redistributed), conditional continuation (specific conditions for 90 more days), or formal re-scoping (the use case is redefined with updated criteria).
Architecture

attrs.alt: AI portfolio kill criteria and sunset process diagram

content.text: Left column: the five categories of kill criteria that trigger sunset review. Right column: the steps in the sunset process when a criterion is met. The process is automatic when criteria are triggered — no individual needs to advocate for stopping the project.
When to use it

Kill criteria must be defined at the start of each project, before significant investment is made. They should be included in the project charter and approved by the steering committee at project inception. Kill criteria are inappropriate to define retroactively — applying criteria to a project after it is underperforming creates the appearance of governance without its substance. Review all kill criteria at each annual portfolio cycle and update them to reflect current business context.
How to implement

Define criteria per tier: Invest tier criteria are ROI-focused (benefit realization rate, adoption rate); Incubate tier criteria are learning-focused (hypotheses tested, learning artifacts produced); Research tier criteria are exploration-focused (useful insights generated, technology risk identified).
Set concrete thresholds: each criterion must have a specific, observable threshold — '50% of projected benefit realized by month 18' not 'adequate progress toward business value'.

Specify review triggers and governance: document who reviews when a criterion is met, what evidence is required, what decision authority exists (remediate vs sunset vs re-scope), and what the timeline is for the remediation period.
Include criteria in project charter: kill criteria are not an appendix — they appear in the executive summary of the project charter, signed by the sponsor.

Build criterion monitoring into reporting: milestone dashboards should include kill criterion status (green/amber/red) for every active project; an amber criterion that has been amber for three months without remediation should auto-escalate.

Document sunset decisions: when a project is sunsetted, write a decision record: which criterion triggered it, what the remediation attempt showed, and what the team and artifacts will be used for next.
Examples

# Sunset process executor: actions when a project is formally sunsetted

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date

@dataclass
class SunsetAction:
    action: str
    owner: str
    deadline_days: int
    notes: str

SUNSET_PLAYBOOK = [
    SunsetAction("Archive model artifacts and training data to model registry",
                 "ML Lead", 7, "Tag with sunset date and reason; retain for 24 months per policy"),
    SunsetAction("Produce sunset retrospective document",
                 "Product Lead", 14, "Key learnings, what worked, what did not, for future analogous projects"),
    SunsetAction("Communicate sunset to business unit sponsor",
                 "Program Director", 3, "Written communication with decision rationale and portfolio redirect plan"),
    SunsetAction("Redirect team capacity to next prioritized use case",
                 "Program Director", 14, "Team transition plan; avoid 'limbo' periods between projects"),
    SunsetAction("Update portfolio register and roadmap",
                 "Portfolio Manager", 7, "Remove from active portfolio; record in sunset log with date and trigger criterion"),
    SunsetAction("Decommission production infrastructure",
                 "Platform Lead", 30, "Remove from LLM gateway; terminate vector store; revoke API keys"),
]

def execute_sunset(project_name: str, trigger_criterion: str, decision_date: date):
    print(f"SUNSET ACTIVATED: {project_name}")
    print(f"Trigger: {trigger_criterion}")
    print(f"Decision date: {decision_date}\nActions:")
    for i, action in enumerate(SUNSET_PLAYBOOK, 1):
        deadline = date(decision_date.year, decision_date.month, decision_date.day)
        print(f"  {i}. [{action.owner}] {action.action}")
        print(f"     Due: {action.deadline_days} days | Notes: {action.notes}")

execute_sunset("Customer churn prediction agent",
               "Remediation period expired: benefit realization still at 32%, target was 50%",
               date(2025, 11, 2))
Real-world example

A global bank had 14 active AI projects after 18 months of its program. Three had been informally acknowledged as 'stalled' by the delivery team but no formal action had been taken — they were absorbing $280K monthly in combined platform costs and one full-time ML engineer each. The program director introduced kill criteria retroactively by converting the informal performance reviews into a formal criterion evaluation. All three projects failed the benefit realization criterion (all were below 30% at month 18). Two entered remediation with 60-day plans. One had no credible remediation path and was sunset.

The sunsetted project freed two ML engineers and $90K monthly in infrastructure costs. Those resources were immediately redirected to a use case that had been in the Invest tier queue for 4 months awaiting capacity. The redirected project delivered its first production deployment in month 22 — earlier than any of the three stalled projects would have delivered even under optimistic remediation scenarios.
Common mistakes
Defining criteria after performance problems are visible: retroactively applied kill criteria are a rationalization, not governance — criteria must be defined at project inception.
Setting only ROI criteria: projects may fail for non-financial reasons (data unavailability, regulatory change); criteria must cover all major failure modes.
Not including criteria in the project charter: kill criteria that live only in a program management spreadsheet are not visible to sponsors and are not enforced; they must be in the charter.
Allowing criteria to expire without review: kill criteria set at project inception are not automatically valid 24 months later; they must be reviewed at each phase gate.

Using the sunset process punitively: sunset is a portfolio management tool, not a performance management tool; the sunset decision should be communicated as 'the portfolio prioritization requires redirecting these resources' not 'this team failed'.
Best practices
Define criteria per portfolio tier: Invest criteria are ROI-focused, Incubate criteria are learning-focused, Research criteria are insight-focused — same framework, different thresholds.

Make criteria mechanical rather than judgmental: 'benefit realization below 50% at month 18' is a mechanical criterion; 'insufficient progress' is a judgmental criterion that generates negotiation rather than governance.
Include a remediation mechanism: a single instance of a missed criterion triggers remediation, not immediate sunset — this prevents premature killing of projects with recoverable delays.

Communicate criteria publicly at project start: when all stakeholders know the kill criteria from day one, sunset decisions are not surprises and cannot be credibly characterized as politically motivated.
Celebrate sunset decisions as good governance: a sunset that frees resources for better-return projects is a success, not a failure — frame it as such in program communications.
Performance and scalability

Kill criteria management scales with the portfolio: a program with 10 active projects can manage criteria manually; a program with 50+ active projects needs automated criterion monitoring integrated into the milestone dashboard. The criterion status (green/amber/red) for every active project should be visible to the program director on a single dashboard, with amber projects automatically triggering a review request to the portfolio manager.

Pre-defined kill criteria (recommended)
Fast: criteria trigger process
Low: criteria are pre-agreed
High: zombie projects prevented
Ad-hoc project review
Slow: requires advocacy
High: political negotiation

Lowest: resources locked in underperformers
Security and reliability

When a project is sunsetted, the security decommissioning process is as important as the resource redirect. Model artifacts must be archived with appropriate access controls (not deleted — they may be needed for audit or future reuse). API keys and access tokens for the sunsetted use case must be revoked within 30 days. Production infrastructure (model endpoints, vector stores) must be terminated per the data retention policy. The sunset decision record must be retained for the program's audit lifecycle — typically 5 years for regulated industries.
Key takeaways
Kill criteria must be defined at project inception, not retroactively — retrospective criteria are rationalization, not governance.
Criteria must be mechanical, not judgmental: specific thresholds tied to observable metrics, not qualitative assessments.
A remediation mechanism prevents premature sunsets: one missed criterion triggers a time-bounded remediation period; only persistent non-recovery triggers sunset review.
Portfolio tier determines criterion type: Invest tier is ROI-focused, Incubate is learning-focused, Research is insight-focused.
Sunset decisions are portfolio management, not performance management — communicate them as resource reallocation, not team failure.
The security decommissioning process (artifact archival, API key revocation, infrastructure termination) is as important as the resource redirect when a project is sunsetted.
